source.title: Human cognition involves the dynamic integration of neural activity and neuromodulatory systems

confidence_rationale: Empirically rigorous task-fMRI paper showing that cognitive transitions are better described as continuous trajectories through a low-dimensional neural manifold than as discrete switches between labelled states. This directly challenges MECH-047's discrete mode-switching architecture and the sharp hysteresis gate. However, the paper also shows that neuromodulator activity (especially LC-NE proxy via pupillometry) predicts position along the manifold, which is partially consistent with MECH-047's arousal-gating component. Net: the continuous manifold finding weakens the discrete-mode assumption; the neuromodulator-position correlation provides partial support for the gating claim.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Task-fMRI during a cognitive flexibility paradigm reveals that brain network configurations change continuously along a low-dimensional trajectory (2-3 principal dimensions) rather than switching discretely between separable states. Position along this trajectory correlates with pupil-linked arousal (LC-NE proxy). Transitions between task conditions produce smooth traversals of manifold space, not sudden jumps. The authors argue that discrete state labels (e.g., task-on / task-off) are coarse summaries of an underlying continuous process.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-047: Shine et al. challenge the discrete three-mode model that MECH-047 presupposes. If neural dynamics are fundamentally continuous, then the REE mode-transition gate is an approximation -- a discretisation of a continuum that may not have sharp boundaries. The failure signature is that there may be no well-defined moment of 'mode entry' at which hysteresis kicks in; instead, hysteresis might be better modelled as a continuous function of position along the arousal/integration manifold. The partial support comes from the finding that pupil-linked arousal predicts manifold position: this is consistent with MECH-047's claim that LC-NE arousal modulates the ease of transitioning, even if the transition is continuous rather than discrete.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The cognitive flexibility paradigm used by Shine et al. is not directly analogous to the REE three-mode cycle (doing, ready-vigilance, default-mode). Manifold dimensionality reduction may obscure discrete attractors if they exist; the paper cannot rule out metastability within the continuous manifold. The pupillometry LC-NE proxy is indirect. The finding is a challenge at the level of mechanistic framing (discrete vs. continuous) not a falsification of hysteresis per se -- hysteresis is possible in continuous systems too, but the MECH-047 implementation assumes discrete mode labels. DOI verified; citation details accurate.
mapping.source_context: Task-fMRI (7T, N=30), cognitive flexibility paradigm with switching between task sets. PCA/manifold analysis of whole-brain connectivity matrices. Pupillometry as LC-NE proxy. No pharmacological manipulation.

confidence_components.notes: High-quality empirical paper; the challenge to discrete-mode framing is methodologically sound and directly relevant. Mapping fidelity is moderate because the paper's manifold model and REE's mode-manager operate at different levels of description. The mixed direction reflects genuine ambiguity: continuous dynamics + neuromodulator gating is not inconsistent with REE if the discrete-mode abstraction is understood as a computational-level approximation.
